ground_truth.title: Making agentic government work: 7 principles for safer, smarter AI adoption
ground_truth.author: Chris Radich
ground_truth.main_content: Federal agencies have spent years modernizing, but modernization has not always made work easier. Many programs added tools without improving how work actually flows. Automation grew, but often in pockets with little coordination, shared data or mission alignment. We are now at the point where automation, machine learning and agentic orchestration can genuinely work together. This is not theory. It is already happening in defense and civilian agencies that have moved past pilots and into production, using agents that bring context, consistency and speed to complex workflows while preserving accountability. These seven principles for an agentic government give leaders a practical framework for adopting automation and AI responsibly. 1. Orchestration Over Silos: Agencies do not need more tools. They need the tools they have working together. Agentic Government starts with orchestration: a single operational fabric that connects human work, automation, machine learning and oversight. 2. Human in the Loop, Not Human Out of the Loop: Agents move quickly, but humans define the mission, approve exceptions and own the outcome. Approval queues are mandatory. 3. Mission Outcomes Over Model Scores: The real measure of AI and automation is whether they improve readiness, auditability, speed, accuracy or service delivery. 4. Transparency Over Black Boxes: Government must be able to explain every digital action. Agentic systems should provide clear, auditable logs, not summaries or approximations. 5. Workforce Enablement Over Workforce Reduction: Agentic automation expands capacity. It frees employees from repetitive tasks so they can focus on analysis, judgment and mission execution. 6. Processes Over Systems: Agentic automation succeeds when it targets the workflow, not the software. If you automate a broken process, it remains broken. 7. Deterministic First, Non-Deterministic Second: Rules-based automations are the foundation. They are consistent, repeatable and explainable. AI adds intelligence on top. Real progress will come from connecting existing systems, expanding RPA, using ML where documents exist and integrating LLMs securely inside orchestrated workflows.
with: Agentic government is not a future concept. It is a practical operational model agencies can adopt today.
Agents move quickly, but humans define the mission, approve exceptions and own the outcome.
If you cannot explain it, you cannot deploy it.
Automate the work, not the worker. Agentic automation expands capacity.
Real progress will not come from experimental models sitting in labs. It will come from connecting existing systems.
